output.raw_output:
{
  "title": "Landlord Withholding Your Security Deposit? A Step-by-Step Recovery Guide",
  "meta_description": "Your landlord is withholding your security deposit illegally. Learn your tenant rights, what landlords can deduct, and how to demand your money back in writing.",
  "content_outline": [
    "Why Your Landlord Is Withholding Your Security Deposit (And Why They Might Be Wrong)",
    "Tenant Rights: What a Landlord Can and Cannot Deduct",
    "How Long Does a Landlord Have to Return Your Deposit? State-by-State Deadlines",
    "Writing a Security Deposit Dispute Letter That Actually Works",
    "What to Do When Your Landlord Won't Return Your Deposit: Next Steps"
  ],
  "key_points": [
    "Most states require landlords to return deposits within 14–30 days and provide an itemized deduction list.",
    "Normal wear and tear, carpet cleaning, and minor scuffs are not legally deductible in most states.",
    "A formal demand letter is often the fastest, cheapest way to recover a withheld security deposit before going to court.",
    "If a landlord misses the legal deadline or fails to itemize, they may forfeit the right to keep any portion of the deposit."
  ],
  "cta": "Stop waiting. Use DepositBack to generate a state-specific security deposit demand letter in minutes and get your money back.",
  "target_audience": "Frustrated US renters whose landlords are wrongfully withholding or failing to return security deposits, and who need clear legal guidance and fast action.",
  "word_count_target": 800,
  "email_sequence": [
    {
      "email_number": 1,
      "subject_line": "Your landlord can't just keep your deposit (here's why)",
      "preview_text": "If your landlord is withholding your security deposit, they need to prove it. Most don't.",
      "target_keyword": "landlord withholding security deposit",
      "focus": "Problem awareness and rights education",
      "body_copy": "Hi [First Name],\n\nStill waiting for your security deposit?\n\nIf your landlord is withholding your security deposit—or simply hasn't returned it within your state's legal deadline—they're betting you won't do anything about it.\n\nHere's the truth: In most states, landlords must return your deposit within 14 to 30 days. If they plan to deduct anything, they must provide an itemized list of charges. Missing paint chips and carpet wear from normal use? Not your financial responsibility.\n\nYet thousands of tenants every year let landlords keep money that legally belongs to them.\n\nOver the next few days, I'll show you exactly what your landlord can and cannot deduct, how long they really have to pay you back, and the simple letter that forces their hand.\n\nTalk soon,\nThe DepositBack Team"
    },
    {
      "email_number": 2,
      "subject_line": "What can a landlord actually deduct from your deposit?",
      "preview_text": "Spoiler: 'Normal wear and tear' is NOT on the list
output.parse_error: Unterminated string starting at: line 32 column 23 (char 2865)
